speaker: interviewer
text: [SIDE CONVERSATIONS] [HIGH TONE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Israel in Haifa in 1985. We were visiting our sister. And this is my sister, Katie. This is myself. This is her daughter, Judith. This is my wife, Ruth, and this is my son, Jeremy-- our son, Jeremy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister was also in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister was also in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came in '43 in June until the liberation [INAUDIBLE] and then she came to-- I found her, and she came from Poland to Germany to live with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you find her?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she going to be interviewed, do you know? You might want to go back over that picture because I was still running time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I didn't find her. Freddie Diamond came to me to visit, to Wiesbaden. He said, Sieg, I met a girl by the name Halbreich. How does she look?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Oswiecim, later Auschwitz, before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are-- what are we looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the marketplace, and you can see there is a name. It means-- it's called Apotheke. It means pharmacy, and I worked there for a short time in this pharmacy. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture was taken right away after the liberation by the American Army, and this shows the entrance of the underground tunnels, where the Germans manufactured these V-1 and V-2 missiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is located where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is located in Nordhausen, under the camp Dora-Nordhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is an entrance to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the tunnels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Underground, sure. This presents the part of the tunnels where the shops-- the workshops-- were.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the missiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where the people were working, yeah, preparing the bombs, the missiles. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture represents members of the Akiba, and we had just at this time, a conference, and I can give you the names. This is Bronek Koshe, who left in '38, Poland and immigrated to Palestine. He is still alive, and I see him and I'm in touch with him. This girl is alive, and she lives in Tel Aviv. Her name is [? Manya. ?] This man was a leader of the Akiba in Poland. He lived in Krakow, and he was one of the resistance fighters against the Germans. And he is very well known. And one day, he dressed up as an SS man and as a chauffeur, as a driver, and he took SS officers on a ride. I don't know where they went, and he shot them all. And he later on was caught and, naturally, was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Adolf-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just said it-- Lieberskind [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This girl is the older sister of [? Manya. ?] Her name is Regina, and she is not alive anymore. And this is myself. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This girl was my fiance before the war, and in 1938-- in '39, in spring, I sent her to Palestine in order I will follow soon. But I did not go because I was involved in so many activities. For instance, I was in charge of the illegal immigration from Poland to Israel-- to Palestine-- and they didn't let me go. They said, Sieg, you're not finished yet, and for months to months they delayed it until the war broke out, and I got stuck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Rella Bitter. Rella is Rahel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she's still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't see each-- yeah. yeah. We didn't see each other anymore, because the war came, and I was in the camps, and after the war, I called my friends in-- in-- the time in Palestine, and they were-- they couldn't believe that I am alive. And we talked a little, and then I asked them, what is Rahel doing? And he hesitated and later said, you know, Sieg, for six years she was waiting for you. Last month, it means in March 1945, she married, and I was liberated in April. And that's the reason I didn't go to-- to Palestine. But I saw her later on. I was about seven, eight times in Israel. In 1960, I met her again. She was, in the meantime, divorced already for many years, and that's all I can say, because I was married. I had children. And she met my wife and the children at that time, too. And she-- every time I went to Israel, I naturally saw her. And she died about, I would say six, seven years ago. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my wife when I met her in Paris. After I sent her to Paris from Germany, I went in the-- in fall, in October '45 to visit her, and this was the first picture taken. And her name is Ruth. So that's the same, and this is already--
